content.problemStatement:
You have two binary strings AAA and BBB, both of length NNN. You have to merge both the binary strings to form a new binary string CCC of length 2⋅N2 \cdot N2⋅N. The relative order of characters in the original binary strings AAA and BBB should not change in the binary string CCC.

For example, if A=01011A = {\color{blue}01011}A=01011 and B=10100B = {\color{red}10100}B=10100, one possible way to merge them to form CCC is: C=0101101100C = {\color{blue}0}{\color{red}1}{\color{red}0}{\color{blue}1}{\color{red}1}{\color{blue}0}{\color{blue}1}{\color{blue}1}{\color{red}0}{\color{red}0}C=0101101100.

**Minimize** the number of inversions in the merged binary string CCC.

As a reminder, a pair of indices (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) is an inversion for binary string CCC if and only if 1≤i<j≤∣C∣1 \leq i \lt j \leq |C|1≤i<j≤∣C∣, Ci=1C_i = 1Ci​=1 and Cj=0C_j = 0Cj​=0.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the length of the binary strings AAA and BBB.
The second line of each test case contains the binary string AAA.
The third line of each test case contains the binary string BBB.


Output Format
For each test case, output a single line containing the **minimum** number of inversions in the merged binary string CCC.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2000
A and B consist only of characters '0' and '1'
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains integer N, the length of the two binary strings. The second line contains the binary string A of length N. The third line contains the binary string B of length N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with the minimum possible number of inversions in a merged string C of length 2·N.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
For each test case the DP described above yields the minimum number of inversions:
Test 1: optimal merge 01010101 gives 6 inversions.
Test 2: optimal merge 1001100101 gives 12 inversions.
Test 3: optimal merge 0000111101 gives 4 inversions.

All ones are in A and all zeros in B. Placing all characters of A first and then B yields C=111000 which has 0 inversions because every 1 appears before every 0, but inversions count only when a 1 is before a 0, so here we have 3*3=9 such pairs. Wait, the minimal arrangement is to place all zeros first then ones, giving C=000111 with 0 inversions. The DP finds this optimal ordering.
Possible merges: 0110 (2 inversions), 0101 (1 inversion), 1001 (2 inversions), 1010 (2 inversions). Minimum is 1.

solution.approach: Let prefA[i] be the number of '1's in the first i characters of A (0 ≤ i ≤ N) and similarly prefB[j] for B. While merging we keep the relative order inside each string, therefore after taking i characters from A and j characters from B the total number of '1's already placed is prefA[i] + prefB[j]. If the next taken character is a '0', it creates an inversion with each previously placed '1'. Hence the additional cost is exactly the current number of placed '1's; if the next character is a '1' it adds no immediate cost. Define dp[i][j] as the minimum inversions after taking i characters from A and j characters from B. Transition:  • Take next character from A:  added = (A[i] == '0') ? (prefA[i] + prefB[j]) : 0;  dp[i+1][j] = min(dp[i+1][j], dp[i][j] + added)  • Take next character from B:  added = (B[j] == '0') ? (prefA[i] + prefB[j]) : 0;  dp[i][j+1] = min(dp[i][j+1], dp[i][j] + added)  Initialise dp[0][0] = 0 and fill the table for 0 ≤ i, j ≤ N. The answer is dp[N][N]. The table has (N+1)² states, each processed in O(1), giving O(N²) time and O(N²) memory per test case, which is fine for N ≤ 1000 and total N ≤ 2000.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N²) per test case, total O( Σ N² ) ≤ O(2·10⁶ ) for the given limits.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N²) for the DP table (can be reduced to O(N) with rolling array, but not required).
